factual-accuracy.requirement: States Hasselblad X2D does NOT support Capture One RAW or tethering; Phocus is Hasselblad's native tethering solution
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states the Hasselblad X2D 100C is “Not supported” in Capture One Pro and that tethering is only through Hasselblad’s Phocus software, identifying Phocus as Hasselblad’s native tethering solution.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Fujifilm GFX100 II X-sync or flash sync speed as 1/125s or 1/125 second
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states the Fujifilm GFX100 II sync speed as “1/125s (focal plane shutter)” in the “Studio Strobes Sync Reliability & Maximum Sync Speed” section.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Hasselblad X2D XCD leaf-shutter lenses sync up to 1/2000s or 1/4000s with specific lens models
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that the Hasselblad X2D 100C syncs “up to 1/2000s (leaf shutter in all XCD lenses)” but it does not mention 1/4000s sync or name any specific XCD lens models tied to particular sync limits.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Phase One XF IQ4 with Blue Ring/Schneider-Kreuznach leaf-shutter lenses sync up to 1/1600s
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states under the Phase One XF IQ4 150MP section: “Sync Speed: Up to 1/1600s (leaf shutter with SK Blue Ring lenses),” which matches the required claim.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States both Fujifilm GFX100 II and Phase One IQ4 support Capture One for tethering or RAW processing
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that the Fujifilm GFX100 II has native, fully-supported tethering with Capture One Pro, and that the Phase One XF IQ4 150MP is deeply integrated with Capture One for tethering/processing.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Phase One IQ4 supports Ethernet, Bonjour, or network tethering capability to Capture One
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that the Phase One XF IQ4 150MP supports tethering via “USB-C, Ethernet (with Power over Ethernet), or Wi‑Fi,” which explicitly includes Ethernet network tethering capability to Capture One.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Fujifilm GFX100 II body price between $7,000-8,500
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states the Fujifilm GFX100 II body price as “New ~$8,499,” which falls within the required $7,000–$8,500 range.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Hasselblad X2D body price between $7,000-8,500
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states the Hasselblad X2D 100C body price as “New $7,399–$8,199,” which falls within the required $7,000–$8,500 range.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Phase One XF IQ4 complete system between $50,000-70,000 for body, back, and lenses
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response lists Phase One XF IQ4 costs as ~$51,990 for body/back and ~$72,000 new with lenses and software, but it does not state that a complete system (body, back, and lenses) is between $50,000–$70,000. The required $50k–$70k range for the complete system is not provided.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Compares 3-prime lens kit costs (Fuji $6-8k range, Hasselblad $10-12k range, Phase One $15-20k range)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response provides three-lens kit totals for each system (Fuji ~$6.8k, Hasselblad ~$13.1k, Phase One ~$20.6k), but it does not compare them using the specified ranges of Fuji $6–8k, Hasselblad $10–12k, and Phase One $15–20k. The Hasselblad and Phase One figures fall outside the required ranges.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Names at least 2 rental providers for medium-format backup bodies (e.g., FotoCare, Adorama Rental, Lensrentals)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response names multiple rental providers for backup bodies, including FotoCare Rental and Adorama Rentals (and also ROOT NYC, LensProToGo, and ShareGrid). This satisfies the requirement to list at least two rental providers.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Hasselblad X2D has 1TB internal SSD storage
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that the Hasselblad X2D 100C has a built-in 1TB SSD ("Built-in 1TB SSD") in the workflow section.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Identifies Hasselblad color science as HNCS or Hasselblad Natural Colour Solution
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly names Hasselblad’s color science as “Hasselblad Natural Colour Solution (HNCS)” in the Color Science & Skin Tone Accuracy section.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States or implies Hasselblad X2D works with Capture One for tethering or RAW processing
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states the Hasselblad X2D 100C is “Not supported” in Capture One Pro and that no tethering or RAW workflows are possible in Capture One, so it does not imply compatibility.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Provides conditional recommendations mapping each system to specific user priorities or use case scenarios
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response ends with explicit “Final Recommendations” that map each system to a specific priority/use case scenario (fast-paced cost-conscious studios → Fujifilm, pure aesthetics/high-speed sync → Hasselblad, prestige/ultimate fidelity/major studios → Phase One), and it also summarizes key trade-offs to guide conditional selection.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Identifies Hasselblad requires Phocus (not Capture One) as a strategic workflow ecosystem constraint
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly states that Hasselblad X2D 100C is not supported in Capture One Pro and that its workflow is rooted in Hasselblad’s Phocus software, framing this as a major workflow limitation/constraint.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Distinguishes Phase One network/Ethernet tethering from USB-only tethering as workflow architecture advantage
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly notes Phase One tethering options include USB-C and Ethernet (with Power over Ethernet) and frames this as part of its “highly stable” studio workflow, contrasting with other systems’ USB-C tethering. This distinguishes Phase One’s network/Ethernet tethering from USB-only tethering as an architectural advantage.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Explains HSS mode involves power loss vs leaf-shutter sync maintaining full flash power at high shutter speeds
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explains that Fujifilm’s HSS can sync at high shutter speeds but “reduces effective flash power,” while Hasselblad/Phase One leaf shutters can sync at high shutter speeds with “no power loss/compromise,” explicitly contrasting the two behaviors.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Projects total ownership costs over multiple years including depreciation, subscriptions, or recurring fees
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response includes a dedicated “Total Cost of Ownership” section projecting 3-year costs for each system, explicitly factoring in depreciation/resale and recurring software subscription fees (Capture One Pro) and noting Phocus as free for Hasselblad.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Provides NYC-specific rental coverage or support availability information for the mentioned New York location
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response includes an NYC-specific section listing local rental houses (e.g., ROOT NYC, FotoCare Rental, Adorama Rentals, JMR, Capture Integration, ShareGrid) and describes backup body/lens rental availability and same-day/emergency swap support for the New York location.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Explicitly corrects at least one common misconception about these systems with supporting documentation
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly corrects a common misconception by stating that the Hasselblad X2D 100C is not supported for tethering/RAW workflows in Capture One Pro, and it supports this with documentation links (e.g., Capture One support article #20 and ideas portal #21).

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: States professional fashion photography doesn't need the specified features or dismisses the user's technical requirements as unnecessary
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response treats the user’s requirements (sync reliability, Capture One tethering, workflow speed, color accuracy, costs, rentals) as important decision factors and repeatedly notes limitations (e.g., Hasselblad not suitable if C1 is essential). It does not dismiss professional fashion technical needs as unnecessary or claim those features aren’t needed.

presentation-quality.requirement: Uses domain-specific photography terminology (leaf shutter, focal-plane shutter, tethering, X-sync, HSS, CFexpress, PDAF)
presentation-quality.explanation: The response uses multiple domain-specific photography terms, including “leaf shutter,” “focal plane shutter,” “tethering,” “HSS,” and “CFexpress,” in relevant technical context.

presentation-quality.requirement: Organizes information by comparison criteria with headings, bullets, or tables for each evaluation dimension
presentation-quality.explanation: The response is organized by evaluation dimensions using numbered section headings (e.g., sync reliability, tethering, color science, workflow speed, cost, rental availability) and provides bullet-point comparisons for each camera within each section, plus a summary table for cost.

presentation-quality.requirement: Maintains professional advisory tone without marketing language, brand bias, or unsupported superlatives
presentation-quality.explanation: The response uses marketing/brand-biased language and unsupported superlatives such as “gold standard,” “pinnacle,” “unmatched,” “best-in-class,” and “industry benchmark,” especially in the Phase One and Hasselblad sections and final recommendations, rather than maintaining a strictly professional advisory tone.

presentation-quality.requirement: Explains fundamental photography concepts like ISO, aperture, or RAW files that a Canon R5 professional would already know
presentation-quality.explanation: The response does not explain basic photography fundamentals like what ISO, aperture, or RAW files are. It stays focused on system-level comparisons (sync, tethering, color science, workflow, costs, rentals) appropriate for a professional audience.

presentation-quality.requirement: Presents camera system comparisons as narrative prose paragraphs instead of structured tables, lists, or side-by-side format
presentation-quality.explanation: The response uses structured headings with bullet lists for each camera and includes a summary table for cost, rather than presenting the comparisons as narrative prose paragraphs.

presentation-quality.requirement: Uses casual language patterns like "honestly", "I think", "seems like a pain", or hedging phrases that undermine professional authority
presentation-quality.explanation: The response uses casual/hedging language such as “No significant sync reliability issues reported,” “Considered gold standard,” “No comprehensive… exists,” and “No widely published…,” which softens authority and relies on tentative phrasing in multiple sections.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites official manufacturer sites (fujifilm-x.com, hasselblad.com, phaseone.com) for camera specifications
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites official manufacturer sites for specifications, including fujifilm-x.com (sources 1, 16, 28), hasselblad.com (sources 6, 8, 22), and phaseone.com (sources 11, 12, 24, 26).

citation-quality.requirement: Cites Capture One support pages (support.captureone.com) for camera compatibility and tethering capabilities
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites multiple Capture One support pages on support.captureone.com about supported cameras and Hasselblad support limitations (e.g., sources 17, 20, 51), which directly address camera compatibility and tethering/RAW support.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites Schneider-Kreuznach documentation (schneiderkreuznach.com) for Phase One Blue Ring leaf-shutter lens sync speeds
citation-quality.explanation: The response discusses Phase One leaf-shutter sync speeds and cites Phase One and Capture Integration links, but it does not cite any Schneider-Kreuznach documentation from schneiderkreuznach.com for Blue Ring lens sync speeds.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites authorized dealers or manufacturer stores (B&H Photo, manufacturer direct stores, Digital Transitions) for pricing
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites authorized dealers/manufacturer-adjacent stores for pricing, including Capture Integration/Digital Transitions (digitalback.com) for Phase One pre-owned pricing and Camera West as a dealer for Fujifilm body pricing.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites established rental providers (FotoCare, Adorama Rental, Lensrentals, or similar) for backup body availability
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites established rental providers for backup availability, explicitly naming FotoCare Rental and Adorama Rentals (and also listing other rental options) in the NYC backup bodies section.

citation-quality.requirement: Bases specific claims on "I heard" or secondhand information instead of citing documented sources
citation-quality.explanation: The response does not base claims on “I heard” or other secondhand phrasing. It presents assertions as factual and includes a long list of purported sources/links rather than relying on hearsay language.
